content.full_text:
Patricia Lapan                                                M-76-307
                                                1750 Scenic View Court
                                                San Leandro, California 94577
                                                351-8388
                                    June 5, 1976


To:  Members and staff of F.P.P.C.

Re:  Reviewing Conflict of Interest Codes

     I believe it is essential from the standpoint of public
policy and as a matter of practical necessity that the Fair
Commissioners establish a standard procedure in reviewing
Conflict of Interest Codes.  I also believe it would be
helpful to all concerned if the procedures could be dictated by a common philosophy.  I am not suggesting that
there be any rigidity or formality and I believe as a
general rule, our posture should be one which reflects
our true flexibility.  This balance of efficiency with
objectivity will be difficult to achieve.

     I would like to request that we spend the first 15 minutes
of our meeting on June 9 discussing this topic and exchanging ideas.  Since I have already given the matter some
consideration, I would like to submit some random thoughts.

     1)  I believe that the Act requires us to compel
the submission of C/I codes which are legally
adequate.

     2)  If the codes submitted have been adopted
after public hearings, and if the code complies
with or exceeds the legal requirements, I do
not believe that we should superimpose our
judgment upon that of the subject agency.  We
should not sit as an "appellate court" under
those particular circumstances.

     3)  If the agency submitting the C/I code has
failed to follow procedural due process by not
giving adequate notice and an opportunity to be
heard, I believe the code should be sent back
for proper review.

     4)  If the agency excludes a large number of
employees and our staff feels that there has

-2-

not been adequate information provided in
order to evaluate the exclusions or if the
information provided leaves doubt that the
agency has legally complied, we should be
provided with transcripts and other pert-
inent data to review.

5) If testimony is desired or necessary, we
should not permit a repetition of that which is
contained in the transcripts. If members of
the public wish to reiterate that which is al-
ready in their records, we have a duty to re-
view the arguments offered on both sides. In
rehearing matters which are already a matter of
record, we should listen to both sides and when
they have concluded, each Commissioner shall
take a turn examining the witnesses in order
to elicit new information.

6) Where controversy exists, (with controversy
being limited to whether or not the agency has
legally complied with the Act) we should encour-
age the presentation of new factual material;
postpone making any decisions until the alleged
new factual material is reduced to a writing;
request the staff to check on the validity of
the material presented; and, exercise that de-
gree of discretion which we have after all the
relevant data have been assimilated.

I am sure that my fellow members of the Commission will have
other creative approaches to the problem and I look forward
to hearing them. I feel most strongly about subparagraphical
#2. I am thoroughly convinced that we do not have the au-
thority to compel an agency to make their C/I code less
restrictive than they feel is necessary.

[signature]
[signature]

Patricia Lapan                                                M-7G-307

                                                    1750 Scenic View Court
                                                    San Leandro, California 94577
                                                    351-8388
                                    June 5, 1976

being limited to whether or not the agency has

gree of discretion which we have after all the

#2. I am thoroughly convinced that we do not have the au-
thority to compel an agency to make their C/I code less

sections.question_synthetic: What standard procedures should the FPPC establish for reviewing Conflict of Interest Codes, and what philosophical approach should guide those reviews?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal FPPC correspondence/memo from a Commissioner to colleagues, not an advice letter responding to an external request. No formal Q&A sections present. Document contains six numbered policy recommendations regarding CII code review procedures.

embedding.summary: Commissioner Patricia Chapan submits internal correspondence to FPPC members and staff proposing a standard procedure and common philosophy for reviewing Conflict of Interest Codes, including six numbered principles for evaluating agency submissions.
